brief: KNOWLEDGE GAP / WHY IT MATTERS: - AF patients on oral anticoagulant therapy (OAT) who still suffer stroke/systemic embolism (SSE) without a vascular cause, OR who develop LAA thrombus (LAAT), are defined as having 'malignant LAA'. - Optimal strategy to reduce SSE risk in these patients is unknown. - Real-world practice patterns across European centres had not been characterized; study aims to map diagnostic and therapeutic pathways for malignant LAA. STUDY DESIGN + SETTING: - Physician opinion survey (cross-sectional questionnaire). - 18-item online questionnaire focused on malignant LAA. - Disseminated by European Heart Rhythm Association (EHRA) Scientific Initiatives Committee; respondents from European cardiac centres. POPULATION / SAMPLE / N: - n = 196 physicians participated. - Respondents = practicing cardiologists/EP physicians at European centres (not patients). INTERVENTION / COMPARISON: - No intervention; descriptive survey of reported practice. - Compared relative preference/ranking of diagnostic tools and treatment steps. KEY RESULTS (numbers as given): - Sample size: 196 physicians (no other quantitative percentages, p-values, CIs, or effect sizes reported in abstract). - High reported confidence in transoesophageal echocardiography (TEE) for diagnosing LAAT. - Preferred FIRST-line treatment step for malignant LAA: switching to ANOTHER direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC). - Subsequent ranked options: switch to vitamin K antagonist (VKA), low-molecular-weight heparin, or continued/optimized DOAC dosage. - LAA closure (LAAC) ranked as the LAST option overall. - LAAC considered viable in patients with embolic stroke despite OAT and NO thrombus on TEE ('empty LAA') after comprehensive workup excluding other embolic sources. - In practice majority treated as OUTPATIENTS: shifting VKA->DOAC or DOAC->DOAC. - LAAC reserved for patients with higher bleeding risk or complications of malignant LAA (e.g., stroke). MAIN TAKEAWAY: - Snapshot of contemporary European management of malignant LAA: drug-switching (especially DOAC-to-DOAC) is the dominant first-line approach, mostly managed on an outpatient basis; LAAC is a last-resort reserved for high bleeding risk or complicated cases.
